text: The posterior rectus sheath is visible as the surgeon begins insufflation, expanding the surgical field for the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon uses a telescope to perform dissection for a TEP inguinal hernia repair, proceeding without the use of a balloon.
The surgeon identifies the correct plane and carefully enters between the two layers of the transversalis fascia during the TEP repair for inguinal hernia.
The surgeon navigates through the avascular space, characterized by visible coir waves, with no blood vessels present.

The surgeon carefully dissects while ensuring the muscle remains covered by a thin layer of fascia, avoiding excessive posterior exposure. The fascia and sheath are visible, and the dissection is maintained just above these structures.

The surgeon identifies a branch of the inferior epigastric vessel while attempting to find the correct plane. The wrong plane is noted with visible muscle and vessels. The posterior rectus sheath is visible below, and the surgeon navigates between the two layers of the transversalis fascia, moving lower towards the pubic region.

The surgeon visualizes the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, indicating the target area has been reached. The space is expanded on both sides, with careful forward and backward movements of the telescope to avoid trauma to the peritoneum. Vessels and the dissected plane are clearly identifiable.

The surgeon opens up the lateral space, revealing a direct hernia on the right side and an indirect hernia on the left. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, positioned in the roof, with the edge of the peritoneum seen below.
The surgeon uses a needle to confirm the position, ensuring the port does not penetrate the peritoneum, before inserting a 5mm port just lateral to the rectus muscle.
The surgeon uses a low profile hook to manipulate tissue within the surgical field, focusing on the area around the hernia repair site.
The surgeon uses scissors with a low voltage of current to minimize the presence of fumes during the dissection.

The surgeon uses cautery to manage small thin vessels, ensuring the preservation of tissue planes and maintaining optimal lighting in the surgical field to prevent image darkening from bleeding or hematoma.
The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, visibly separating tissue layers with precision.
The Cooper's ligament becomes visible in the surgical field as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon continues dissection approximately three centimeters below the pubic bone, ensuring adequate space for mesh placement. The dissection progresses towards the midline, using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques to detach tissue attachments and extend the dissection to the opposite side.

The surgeon prepares to introduce the working port on the opposite side, positioning it one to two centimeters below the umbilicus and lateral to the rectus abdominis muscle. This alignment ensures proper placement of the mesh during the hernia repair.
The surgeon gradually enters the opposite side, revealing the rectus muscle. There is visible fibrosis in the area, possibly due to a previous episode of irreducibility.

The surgeon continues dissection, ensuring to stay within the correct anatomical planes while approaching the opposite side. The inferior epigastric vessels are anticipated to appear soon, and care is taken to leave them positioned on the roof to avoid complicating the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the anterior and posterior fascial layers of the rectus muscle, preserving the small fascia on the rectus muscle. The dissection is carried out using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques, maintaining the correct plane despite some bleeding.
The surgeon uses two instruments to perform traction and counter-traction for effective dissection in the lateral space, ensuring adequate exposure and separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon continues the hernial dissection and space creation using a single working port, maintaining the surgical field with minimal instrumentation.
The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the left side, visible as a distinct anatomical landmark during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon carefully identifies the correct tissue planes, using electrocautery sparingly to minimize tissue damage during the dissection.
The surgical plane gradually opens up, revealing the underlying tissue layers as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessel, carefully dissecting near it to avoid creating a buttonhole defect in the fused transversalis fascia, which could result in loss of pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to identify the edge of the peritoneum, focusing on the medial and lateral spaces before proceeding to dissect the hernia sac.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to manipulate and separate tissue layers, aiding in the identification of anatomical planes during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon continues to open an avascular plane, which visibly expands rapidly with the current maneuver.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum as it extends into the internal ring, forming the hernial sac.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum, which is visible as the boundary of the hernial sac.
The surgeon grasps the tissue and continues dissection to fully open the lateral space of Bogro's, revealing the anatomical area involved in the hernia repair.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform sharp dissection on the edge of the peritoneum, ensuring it is well away from the internal ring as the hernial sac is exposed.
The surgeon identifies and visually appreciates the edge of the fascia and the peritoneum.
The surgeon expands the lateral space of Bogro's further to ensure adequate room for the mesh placement, preparing the area for comfortable seating of the mesh.
The dulux fascia, covering the nerves on the lateral side, is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the indirect hernia sac as it protrudes into the surgical field, visible within the expanded lateral space of Bogro's.
The surgeon applies traction to push away the sling fibers, facilitating comprehensive dissection of the hernia sac.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to push away the remaining sling fibers, gradually dissecting further into the hernia sac.

The surgeon attempts to completely reduce the hernia sac, despite its larger size, aiming to minimize seroma formation. In cases where reduction is challenging due to excessive oozing, the sac may be divided at the internal ring.
The surgeon applies an endo loop or ligature to securely close the divided hernia sac, ensuring that the mesh is positioned extraperitoneally.
The surgeon exposes a wide indirect hernia sac, highlighting its breadth within the surgical field.
The surgeon initiates the dissection of the hernia sac from the lateral side, gradually progressing medially to separate the sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, aiming to separate and protect the core structures, ensuring they are not damaged during the procedure.
The edge of the dissected hernia sac is visible, showing the progress made in separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon grasps the edge of the hernia sac and continues the dissection, moving medially to separate the sac from surrounding structures.
The vas deferens becomes visible as the surgeon continues the dissection around the hernia sac, carefully revealing this important structure.
The surgeon carefully uses electrocautery sparingly to avoid thermal damage to the vas deferens and testicular vessels during the dissection.
The surgeon has dissected around the hernia sac, leaving only a few flimsy attachments visible.
The surgeon uses a short burst of energy to carefully dissect the testicular vessels and the vas deferens, moving them away from the main field of dissection.
The surgeon gradually advances the dissection cranially, continuing to separate the tissue layers in the surgical field.

The surgeon completely encircles and dissects the hernia sac, then proceeds to open the avascular space further, ensuring that important structures like the vas deferens and testicular vessels are preserved and secured.
The surgeon holds the hernia sac in their hand, applying tension to the tissue to facilitate further dissection and identify the fundus of the sac.
The surgeon gradually reveals the fundus of the hernia sac, exposing the transversalis fascia as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon carefully changes positions to avoid injury to the peritoneum while continuing the dissection. Transversalis sling fibers are visible and being dissected away from the hernia sac. The fundus of the sac is gradually becoming more visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the location of the vas deferens and testicular vessels before proceeding with cautery dissection, ensuring these structures are preserved during the hernia repair.
The surgeon displays the entire fundus of the hernia sac, with a few remaining fibers visible. These fibers are carefully dissected to completely free the fundus from surrounding tissue.
The assisting hand applies significant traction to the hernia sac, facilitating smooth laparoscopic dissection. The thin fascia between the sac and surrounding tissues is clearly visible.
The surgeon carefully pushes away the dissected testicular veins, ensuring they are clear from the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the last remaining attachments between the hernia sac and surrounding tissues, ensuring the sac is fully mobilized.
The surgeon carefully avoids the clearly visible vas deferens while using cautery, ensuring it remains undisturbed during the dissection.
The surgical field shows the vas deferens clearly visible and distant from the area being operated on, ensuring it is not at risk during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the removal of the last adhesions present between the hernia sac and surrounding tissues, ensuring a clear separation.
A small fascia is visible, which the surgeon divides to completely free the hernia sac.
The surgeon divides the remaining thin fascia, completing the separation of the hernia sac.
The surgeon completes the division of the fascial attachments, fully freeing the hernia sac.

The surgeon releases the hernia sac, which is pushed inside the abdomen due to carbon dioxide insufflation, making it no longer visible. The peritoneum has been pushed laterally under vision, and the surgeon proceeds to divide the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon dissects additional fibers attached to the anterior abdominal wall to ensure the lateral mesh can be seated comfortably. The bladder with a Foley catheter is visible, along with the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, which is highlighted by the 'white house sign.'

The surgeon identifies the direct hernia, noting that the pubic bone and arch are not clearly visible due to the hernia. Upon releasing the hernia, the full pubic bone and arch become well visualized. The inferior epigastric artery is observed on the right side, and the surgeon stays close to this artery to open up the lateral space effectively.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneal sac on the right side, avoiding grasping due to its thinness. The tissue is pulled medially to avoid the nearby external iliac vein, which appears collapsed due to carbon dioxide insufflation.
The surgeon releases a small fascia, preparing to expose the underlying vein.
The surgeon applies significant traction to pull the tissues towards the medial side, carefully visualizing the structures before dividing them.
The surgeon prepares to reveal the entire external iliac vein, which is located close to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the external iliac vein, visible as a bluish hue beneath the tissue, while carefully avoiding grasping the thin peritoneal sac nearby.
The surgeon observes the collapsed vein, noting the visible venous pulsation within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the external iliac artery and the external iliac vein, highlighting their positions within the surgical field.
The fascia is visible just above the vein, which is identifiable by its bluish hue.
The surgeon holds the edge of the hernia sac and works to open up the lateral space of Bogros on the right side, revealing the anatomical structures in the area.
The surgeon adheres to the correct anatomical planes while manipulating the hernia sac, ensuring precise dissection around the external iliac artery and vein.
The surgeon performs parietalization on the right side, ensuring the mesh will seat comfortably without the lower border being raised away from the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the thin fascia located between the peritoneum and the testicular structures, including the vessels, artery, and vas deferens.
The surgeon divides the fascia and pushes it away, creating a comprehensive space for mesh placement in the hernia repair area.

The surgeon clearly defines the edge of the peritoneum, ensuring that surrounding structures are safely distanced before considering the use of cautery. Blunt dissection is performed, resulting in minor oozing.
The surgeon observes the external iliac vein, noting its pulsation and collapse due to carbon dioxide insufflation, while the surrounding tissue planes appear darker as they absorb light.

The surgeon further opens the lateral space to ensure adequate room for mesh placement, revealing a small peritoneal rent. Despite the rent, there is minimal gas loss due to the insertion of a 3mm port.

In the left hypochondrium at Palmer's point, the surgeon closes a peritoneal rent using an endo loop. The rent is visible in the lateral and medial space, and the closure technique ensures the rent does not impede the dissection process.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to dissect further, clearly exposing the lateral portion of the peritoneum. The dissection aims to open the lateral space adequately for mesh placement, ensuring the peritoneum is visible and well-defined.

The surgeon demonstrates the medial overlap of the mesh in the direct hernia repair, showing the fully mobilized lateral space. The space is prepared to accommodate a fifteen by twelve centimeter mesh, which is the recommended minimum size.
The surgeon ensures that the peritoneum is adequately peritonized, indicating that the peritoneal layer is being properly managed and secured.
The vas deferens is visible entering the puncture site within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the landmark for dissection where the vas deferens turns medially and crosses the obliterated umbilical artery.
The edge of the peritoneum mesh is visible, positioned to allow for comfortable placement without rolling.
The surgeon divides the thin fascia and proceeds with blunt dissection, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon highlights the point where the vas deferens turns medially, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the landmark where the vas deferens turns medially and crosses the obliterated umbilical artery, indicating the extent to which the peritoneum has been mobilized.
The surgeon halts the dissection and parietalization process, indicating the completion of this surgical step.

The surgeon demonstrates the area where the obturator nerve and vessels are visible, highlighting the region for potential obturator hernia dissection. The thin fascia over the vein, previously divided, is further dissected to enhance exposure.
The surgeon continues dissection in an avascular plane, moving caudally to further expose anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses short bursts of monopolar energy or cautery to address fibrous tissue, revealing a white shiny structure in the surgical field.
The obturator nerve is visible alongside the testicular vessels and the vas deferens, forming the anatomical region known as the triangle of doom.
The lower edge of the peritoneum is visible, with the external iliac artery seen through the triangle of doom.

The surgeon carefully navigates medially, clearly visualizing the external iliac artery. The previously divided thin fascia is evident, highlighting the need for caution in this area. The widened indirect ring is visible, indicating where the indirect sac was present and has been completely reduced. The inferior epigastric vessels are seen, with the indirect ring located just lateral to them.
The video shows the defect and the hernia that was present earlier, with visible anatomical structures surrounding the area.
The surgeon has completed the dissection, revealing the anatomical structures within the surgical field, including the external iliac artery and the triangle of doom.
The surgeon identifies the anastomotic veins, known as the corona mortis or circle of death, running between the external iliac vein and the obturator nerve.
The retropubic space of Retzius is visible, having been adequately dissected, showing clear anatomical structures within the area.
The direct hernia on the right side is visible in the surgical field.
The edge of the hernial defect is clearly visible, showing the boundary of the previously dissected area.
The surgeon identifies the external iliac artery on the right side and the vas deferens. Upon retracting the artery, the external iliac vein becomes visible.

The surgeon identifies the bluish external iliac vein on the right side, which collapses and reopens due to carbon dioxide insufflation. The vas deferens is traced as it turns medially into the indirect hernia, with the testicular vessels visible laterally. The entire area is opened, and a 15x12 cm lightweight polypropylene mesh is placed.

The surgeon rolls two-thirds of the lightweight polypropylene mesh and secures it with a small suture, leaving one-third open. This technique aids in handling the mesh within the limited space of the TEP approach, preventing crumpling due to the mesh's lightweight nature.

The surgeon has placed the lightweight polypropylene mesh neatly over the hernial defect without any rolling or folding. A Prolene suture is used to secure the mesh to the Cooper's ligament with an intracorporeal suturing technique.
The surgeon divides the knot and removes it, allowing the polypropylene mesh to be unrolled onto the hernia repair site.

The surgeon manually unrolls the lightweight polypropylene mesh and places it on the anterior abdominal wall. The mesh is visibly well-seated without rolling, indicating adequate section and lateral space dissection.
The surgeon takes a suture bite on the right side of the lightweight polypropylene mesh, securing it to the anterior abdominal wall.
Two 15x12 centimeter meshes are placed on either side of the surgical field, covering the anterior abdominal wall.
The meshes are visibly placed with a good medial overlap, extending beyond two centimeters, ensuring proper coverage in the TEP hernia repair.
